lesson.menu_desc: Telling your story & what you have been doing -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 1 -- Reintroducing Myself at B2
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 1 -- Reintroducing Myself at B2

reading.rtitle: The Long Way Round: Why Mid-Life Reinventions Are Quietly Booming
paras: For decades, the story we told about a career was simple: you trained for one thing in your twenties and you did it until you stopped. That story is fading fast. Across the world, people in their fifties and sixties are quietly reinventing themselves, and many describe the shift not as a crisis but as a turning point they have been waiting years to reach.

The pattern is strikingly consistent. People say they have been taking stock of what truly matters, looking back on a long career path with pride, and looking forward with a fresh perspective. Far from winding down, they are juggling new projects, learning new skills, and discovering interests they never had time for while raising families or building demanding businesses.

What unites them is a sense that they have come a long way and still have a great deal to give. They are not running from their old lives; they are building on them. The most fulfilled, researchers note, are the ones who stay curious and well-rounded -- the ones who keep thriving long after the world expected them to slow down.
reading.source: Adapted from a lifestyle feature, The Atlantic Weekend

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main point about mid-life reinventions?
choices: People in their fifties and sixties mostly experience this stage as a painful crisis.
Many people now treat this stage as a positive turning point and keep growing instead of slowing down.
Reinventing yourself after fifty almost always means abandoning everything you did before.

items: The writer says the old story of a single lifelong career is disappearing.
t
It says that story is fading fast.
Most people in the article describe the shift as a crisis.
f
Many describe it as a turning point, not a crisis.
People say they have been taking stock of what truly matters.
t
The text uses exactly this idea.
According to researchers, the most fulfilled people are the ones who quietly withdraw and rest.
f
The most fulfilled stay curious, well-rounded and keep thriving.
The article calls this stage a turning point rather than a crisis. Which word fits your own experience better, and why?
What have you been taking stock of recently in your own life?
The text says the most fulfilled people stay curious and well-rounded. How do you keep yourself curious?
If you look back on your career path so far, what milestone are you proudest of?
Scenario 1
You meet an old colleague you have not seen in ten years. In two or three sentences, tell them what you have been doing lately and how you have come a long way.
Scenario 2
A younger dentist asks you what the most fulfilling part of your career has been. Answer using a present perfect continuous form.
Scenario 3
At a dinner, someone asks what you are passionate about now, outside of work. Describe one interest you have been developing.
Scenario 4
An old friend assumes you have fully retired and stopped everything. Correct them: explain what you have actually been juggling lately.

rephrase.title: Rewrite each sentence with the present perfect continuous
items: I started learning English a year ago and I am still learning.
have been
She began taking stock of her life and she is still doing it.
has been
We started juggling new projects months ago and we continue.
have been
He began looking back on his career and he is still reflecting.
has been
They started thriving in this new chapter and they continue to.
have been

answerkey.title: Reveal the rephrase answer key
list: I have been learning English for a year.
She has been taking stock of her life.
We have been juggling new projects for months.
He has been looking back on his career.
They have been thriving in this new chapter.
answerkey.note: Form check: have / has + been + verb-ing. Use it for an action that started in the past and is still going on (often with for / since).

analyse.title: How the writer frames the change
items: Find two phrases in the text that show reinvention is seen as positive rather than a loss.
The writer says people are 'building on' their old lives, not 'running from' them. In your own words, what is the difference?
The final sentence describes who stays fulfilled. In one sentence, who is the article quietly encouraging the reader to become?

extra_audio.text: First, she explains that dentistry was a long and demanding career path. Then she reaches a turning point and starts taking stock of what matters. These days, she has been juggling travel, languages and new interests. After that, she calls learning English the most fulfilling part of all. Finally, she says she has come a long way and feels she is just getting started.

listenings.text: So, let me reintroduce myself properly. For most of my life I have been a dentist, and I have loved it. But lately, things have been changing. I have been taking stock of what really matters to me, and I have been slowly stepping back from the clinic. It has been a real turning point. These days I have been travelling more, I have been reading the books I never had time for, and I have been learning English, which has honestly been one of the most fulfilling things of all. I have come a long way, and I genuinely feel I am only just getting started.

I have known Pricila for over twenty years, and I can tell you, she has never been the type to sit still. She has been a brilliant dentist, of course, but that was only ever one chapter. Recently she has been reinventing herself completely. She has been travelling, she has been studying languages, and she has been picking up hobbies she never had time for before. People assume that when you reach this stage you wind down. Pricila is the opposite. She has been thriving, and she has come a long way from the shy young woman I first met all those years ago.
